inheritance-for-shared-behavior.label: Inheritance for Shared Behavior
inheritance-for-shared-behavior.summary: Use a parent class to hold shared setup, then practice the same inheritance idea with catalog classes in a separate Try It domain.

sketch0.title: Why shared setup belongs in one place

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.title: Create the CatalogItem base class
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.prompt: The lesson used a parent transit class. Now practice the same idea with a media catalog. In `models/catalog_item.py`, complete `CatalogItem` so it stores `title` on `self.title` and has `label(self)` return the title. Then finish `main.py` by creating `CatalogItem("Hidden Figures")` and printing its label.
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.hint: Complete the model first, then finish the small main script. The starter is intentionally unfinished.

help.concept: A parent/base class keeps shared setup in one place. Here, CatalogItem is the parent class that future catalog types can inherit from.
help.hint_1: The constructor should save the incoming `title` on `self`.
help.hint_2: The `label()` method should return that stored title.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.starterCode:
from models.catalog_item import CatalogItem

# TODO: create one CatalogItem for "Hidden Figures".
# TODO: print the item label.

main_py.content:
from models.catalog_item import CatalogItem

# TODO: create one CatalogItem for "Hidden Figures".
# TODO: print the item label.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.solutionCode:
from models.catalog_item import CatalogItem

item = CatalogItem("Hidden Figures")
print(item.label())

main_py.content:
from models.catalog_item import CatalogItem

item = CatalogItem("Hidden Figures")
print(item.label())

2.message: Store the title on each CatalogItem object.

3.message: label() should return the stored title.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.title: Create a Book subclass shell
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.prompt: Continue the catalog practice. The `CatalogItem` parent class is provided. In `models/book.py`, make `Book` inherit from `CatalogItem` and leave `pass` inside the class for now. Then finish `main.py` by creating `Book("The Martian")` and printing its title.
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.hint: Use `class Book(CatalogItem):`. The child class can stay empty because it inherits the parent constructor.

help.concept: A subclass can start as a small shell. If it inherits from the base class, it can reuse the constructor and attributes without rewriting them yet.
help.hint_2: Keep `pass` inside the class body for now.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.starterCode:
from models.book import Book

# TODO: create Book("The Martian").
# TODO: print the title stored on that book object.

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book

# TODO: create Book("The Martian").
# TODO: print the title stored on that book object.

models_book_py.content:
from models.catalog_item import CatalogItem


# TODO: make Book inherit from CatalogItem.
class Book:
    pass

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.solutionCode:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("The Martian")
print(book.title)

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("The Martian")
print(book.title)

1.message: Book should be constructible with one title.

2.message: A Book object should have the inherited title attribute.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.title: Use a method inherited by Book
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.prompt: Finish the inheritance pattern. Keep `Book` as a simple child class of `CatalogItem`. Do not write a new `label()` method inside `Book` yet. In `main.py`, create `Book("The Hobbit")` and print `book.label()` to show that the child object can use the parent method.
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.hint: The model files are ready. Your job is to use the inherited method from `main.py`.

help.concept: Inherited behavior means a subclass object can use a method defined in the parent class. That is the reason we moved shared behavior into the base class first.
help.hint_2: If inheritance is set up correctly, `Book` does not need its own `label()` here.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.starterCode:
from models.book import Book

# TODO: create Book("The Hobbit").
# TODO: print the label returned by the inherited method.

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book

# TODO: create Book("The Hobbit").
# TODO: print the label returned by the inherited method.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.solutionCode:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("The Hobbit")
print(book.label())

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("The Hobbit")
print(book.label())

1.message: Create the Book object in main.py.

sc-inheritance-shared-work.title: Choose why a base class helps here
sc-inheritance-shared-work.prompt: Book and Movie both need the same `title` setup. Why is a base class helpful?
sc-inheritance-shared-work.hint: Pick the answer about reusing shared code.

help.concept: Inheritance is helpful when related classes share the same core behavior.
help.hint_1: Look for the choice about storing shared setup once.
help.hint_2: Avoid answers about unrelated classes or identical output forever.

options.a: It prevents any subclass from having its own behavior.
options.b: It stores shared setup once so related subclasses can reuse it.
options.c: It turns every object into the same subclass.
options.d: It removes the need to create objects.

fb-inheritance-subclass-name.title: Complete the parent class name
fb-inheritance-subclass-name.prompt: Fill in the base class name: `class Book([blank1]):`
fb-inheritance-subclass-name.hint: Use the shared class that stores `title`.

help.concept: A subclass names its parent inside the parentheses.
help.hint_1: The base class in this topic is the shared item class.
help.hint_2: Use the exact class name from the lesson.

dr-inheritance-build-order.title: Order the inheritance steps
dr-inheritance-build-order.prompt: Put these steps in a sensible beginner order.
dr-inheritance-build-order.hint: Start with the base class, then connect the subclass, then use it.

help.concept: A clean inheritance setup starts with the shared base class before the subclass uses it.
help.hint_1: The base class must exist before the subclass can inherit from it.
help.hint_2: Using the inherited method happens after the objects exist.

sc-inheritance-method-reuse.title: Spot the inherited behavior
sc-inheritance-method-reuse.prompt: If `Book` inherits from `CatalogItem`, what can happen even when `Book` only contains `pass`?
sc-inheritance-method-reuse.hint: Think about what the base class already provides.

help.concept: A subclass can still use methods and attributes inherited from its base class.
help.hint_1: The base class already defines the shared behavior.
help.hint_2: The answer is about reuse, not copying code by hand.

options.a: A Book object can still use `label()` from CatalogItem.
options.b: A Book object loses access to the title attribute.
options.c: A Book object must redefine every inherited method before running.
options.d: A Book object cannot be created until it overrides `label()`.

sketch-0.title: A parent class holds what the children share

sketch-1.title: A child class can start small
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
Once the parent class exists, a child class can begin as a tiny shell:

```python
class TransitItem:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

class BikeStation(TransitItem):
    pass
```

Read `class BikeStation(TransitItem):` as:

> BikeStation is a kind of TransitItem.

`pass` means the child class exists, but it does not add anything new yet. It can still use the parent constructor.

```python
station = BikeStation("Downtown Bikes")
```

That object is a `BikeStation`, but the starting setup came from `TransitItem`.

sketch-2.title: Child objects can use parent methods
sketch-2.bodyMarkdown:
A child object can also use a method defined on the parent class.

```python
class TransitItem:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

    def label(self):
        return self.name

class BikeStation(TransitItem):
    pass

station = BikeStation("Downtown Bikes")
print(station.label())
```

`BikeStation` does not write its own `label()` method, but `station.label()` still works because Python looks up the method on the parent class.

When reading inheritance code, use this mental map:

1. Start with the child class.
2. If the method is not there, look up to the parent class.
3. If the parent has it, the child object can use it.
